questions.question:
An operating system contains 3 user processes each requiring 2 units of resource R. The minimum number of units of R such that no deadlock will ever arise is
A. 3
B. 5
C. 4
D. 6
questions.to_find: Minimum units of resource R needed to guarantee no deadlock among 3 processes each needing 2 units.

understand.plain: We have 3 processes, and each one needs at most 2 units of a single resource R. We want to find the minimum total units of R so that deadlock can never happen, regardless of how the processes request resources.

keywords.explain: A state where a set of processes are each waiting for a resource held by another, so none can proceed.
keywords.example: P1 holds R1 and waits for R2; P2 holds R2 and waits for R1 — both are stuck forever.

keywords.explain: For n processes each needing at most m units, the system is deadlock-free if total units >= n*(m-1)+1, because at least one process can always complete.
keywords.example: 3 processes each needing 2 units: minimum = 3*(2-1)+1 = 4 units.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P1</text><text x='70' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>needs 2</text><rect x='150' y='10' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P2</text><text x='210' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>needs 2</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P3</text><text x='350' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>needs 2</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='400' height='70' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Resource R</text><text x='210' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Min units = n*(max-1)+1 = 3*(2-1)+1 = 4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three processes P1, P2, P3 each needing 2 units of resource R. The minimum total units needed to guarantee no deadlock is computed as 3*(2-1)+1 = 4.

given.aim: Find the minimum total units of resource R to guarantee deadlock freedom for 3 processes each needing at most 2 units.

terms.meaning: Number of processes competing for resource R.
terms.example: P1, P2, P3 are the three processes.
terms.connects: More processes increase the chance of circular wait; n appears in the formula.

terms.meaning: Maximum units of resource R each process may need.
terms.example: Each process can hold at most 2 units before it finishes.
terms.connects: m-1 is the worst-case allocation per process that still leaves it blocked; used in the formula.

terms.meaning: Total resources >= n*(m-1)+1 ensures at least one process can always complete.
terms.example: If total = n*(m-1) = 3, every process holds 1 unit and waits for 1 more — deadlock. Adding 1 unit breaks this.
terms.connects: This is the key formula giving the minimum required units.

given.plan: Apply the standard result: minimum resources for deadlock freedom with n processes each needing at most m units is n*(m-1)+1. Substitute n=3, m=2.

steps.title: Identify worst-case scenario
steps.formula_raw: worst case: each process holds (m-1) units and waits for 1 more
steps.apply: With n=3 processes each needing m=2 units, the worst case is each process holds 1 unit and waits for 1 more. Total held = 3*1 = 3 units. This is a deadlock since no process can complete.
steps.note: If total resources = 3, all processes are stuck. We need one more unit to let at least one process finish.

steps.title: Apply the deadlock-free formula
steps.apply: min_units = 3*(2-1)+1 = 3*1+1 = 4. With 4 units, even if each of the 3 processes holds 1 unit (total=3 held), there is still 1 free unit which can be given to any one process, allowing it to complete and release its units, breaking any potential deadlock.
steps.note: This formula works for any single resource type with n processes and maximum need m.

solution.result: The minimum number of units of R required is 4. Answer: C. 4

questions.formula_note: Deadlock-free resource bound: minimum units = n*(m-1)+1, where n = number of processes and m = maximum resource need per process.

questions.question:
An operating system handles requests to resources as follows. A process (which asks for some resources, uses them for some time and then exits the system) is assigned a unique timestamp when it starts. The timestamps are monotonically increasing with time. Let us denote the timestamp of a process P by TS(P).

When a process P requests a resource the OS does the following:
i. If no other process is currently holding the resource, the OS awards the resource to P.
ii. If some process Q with TS(Q) < TS(P) is holding the resource, the OS makes P wait for the resources.
iii. If some process Q with TS(Q) > TS(P) is holding the resource, the OS restarts Q and awards the resources to P. (Restarting means taking back the resources held by a process, killing it and starting it again with the same timestamp).

When a process releases a resource, the process with the smallest timestamp (if any) among those waiting for the resource is awarded the resource.

A. Can a deadlock occur? If yes, show how. If not prove it.
B. Can a process P ever starve? If yes, show how. If not prove it.
questions.answer: A. No deadlock can occur. B. No starvation can occur.
questions.to_find: Whether deadlock or starvation can occur under this Wait-Die style resource allocation scheme.

understand.plain: This is the 'Wound-Wait' deadlock avoidance scheme (a variant). Older processes (smaller timestamp = higher priority) wound (preempt) younger processes. When an older process P requests a resource held by younger Q, Q is restarted. When a younger process requests a resource held by an older process, the younger process simply waits. This creates a total ordering that prevents circular wait.

keywords.explain: Processes are assigned timestamps at creation; these define a priority order. Resource conflicts are resolved by always favoring the older (smaller timestamp) process.
keywords.example: If P started at time 5 and Q at time 10, TS(P)=5 < TS(Q)=10, so P is older and has higher priority.

keywords.explain: Older process requesting a resource held by a younger process wounds (preempts/restarts) the younger process. Younger process waiting for older simply waits.
keywords.example: P (older) requests resource held by Q (younger): Q is restarted. Q (younger) requests resource held by P (older): Q waits.

keywords.explain: A process waits indefinitely and never gets the resource it needs.
keywords.example: If a process is always preempted by higher-priority processes and never gets to run.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P (older)</text><text x='80' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>TS(P) < TS(Q)</text><rect x='320' y='20' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q (younger)</text><text x='380' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>TS(Q) > TS(P)</text><text x='230' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>requests resource</text><line x1='140' y1='45' x2='320' y2='45' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><defs><marker id='ar' markerWidth='10' markerHeight='7' refX='10' refY='3.5' orient='auto'><polygon points='0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><rect x='20' y='110' width='420' height='40' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='230' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff;font-weight:600;'>Older P requests from younger Q: Q is RESTARTED (wounded)</text><rect x='20' y='155' width='420' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='181' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Younger Q requests from older P: Q WAITS</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two cases: (1) Older process P requests resource held by younger Q — Q is restarted (wounded). (2) Younger process Q requests resource held by older P — Q waits. This prevents circular wait and thus deadlock.

given.aim: Determine if deadlock or starvation is possible under the described Wound-Wait resource allocation policy.

terms.meaning: Timestamp of process P, assigned at start time. Smaller timestamp = older process = higher priority.
terms.example: Process P starting at t=3 has TS(P)=3; process Q starting at t=7 has TS(Q)=7. P is older.
terms.connects: The timestamp ordering is the basis for all resource conflict resolution in this scheme.

terms.term: Rule ii: TS(Q) < TS(P) => P waits
terms.meaning: If the holder Q is older than the requester P, then P (younger) waits. This is the 'wait' part.
terms.example: Q holds R, TS(Q)=2, TS(P)=8. P waits since Q is older.
terms.connects: Younger processes always wait for older ones — no younger process can hold a resource while an older one is waiting, so no circular wait involving an older process.

terms.term: Rule iii: TS(Q) > TS(P) => Q is restarted
terms.meaning: If the holder Q is younger than the requester P, then Q is preempted (wounded/restarted) and P gets the resource.
terms.example: Q holds R, TS(Q)=8, TS(P)=2. Q is restarted; P gets R immediately.
terms.connects: This ensures older (higher priority) processes never wait for younger ones, preventing circular dependency.

given.plan: For deadlock: show that circular wait is impossible because a waiting chain must always go from younger to older process, but there are no cycles in a strict total order. For starvation: when a process is restarted it keeps its original timestamp (not a new one), so it retains its priority and will eventually be the oldest waiting process and get the resource.

steps.title: Prove no deadlock can occur
steps.formula_raw: For deadlock: need cycle P1 waits for P2 waits for ... waits for P1
steps.apply: Under this scheme, P waits for Q only if TS(Q) < TS(P), i.e., Q is strictly older than P. If P1 waits for P2, then TS(P2) < TS(P1). If P2 waits for P3, then TS(P3) < TS(P2). This creates a strictly decreasing chain of timestamps: TS(P1) > TS(P2) > TS(P3) > ... Since timestamps are finite and strictly decreasing, the chain cannot cycle back to P1. Therefore, circular wait is impossible, and deadlock cannot occur.
steps.note: A directed waiting graph under this policy is a DAG (no cycles), which is sufficient to rule out deadlock.

steps.title: Prove no starvation can occur
steps.apply: A process P can be restarted (preempted) only when it is the younger process (TS(P) > TS(requester)). When P is restarted, it is assigned the same original timestamp, NOT a new one. As time progresses and new processes arrive with larger timestamps, P's timestamp becomes relatively older. Eventually, P will be the process with the smallest timestamp among all competing processes and will receive the resource without being preempted. Hence, no starvation occurs.
steps.note: The key insight is that restarted processes retain their original timestamps, preserving their priority relative to newly created processes.

solution.result: A. No deadlock can occur — the waiting relationship induces a strict total order on timestamps, making cycles impossible. B. No starvation can occur — restarted processes keep their original timestamps and eventually become the highest-priority (oldest) waiting process.

questions.formula_note: Wound-Wait deadlock avoidance scheme: older processes preempt (wound) younger ones; younger processes wait for older ones. No circular wait possible due to strict timestamp ordering. No starvation since restarted processes retain original timestamps.

questions.question:
A computer has six tape drives, with n processes competing for them. Each process may need two drives. What is the maximum value of n for the system to be deadlock free?
A. 4
B. 5
C. 3
D. 6
questions.to_find: Maximum number of processes n such that 6 tape drives and each process needing 2 drives guarantees no deadlock.

understand.plain: We have 6 tape drives (total resource units). Each of the n processes can need at most 2 drives. We want the maximum n such that deadlock can never happen. The safe condition requires that total resources > n*(max_need - 1), so there is always at least one process that can complete.

keywords.explain: The system is deadlock-free if: total_resources >= n*(max_need - 1) + 1, equivalently total_resources > n*(max_need - 1).
keywords.example: 6 drives, max_need=2: need 6 > n*(2-1) = n, so n < 6, meaning max n = 5.

keywords.explain: A non-shareable resource that can be held by only one process at a time.
keywords.example: Process P1 holding tape drive 1 means no other process can use it until P1 releases it.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='380' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>6 Tape Drives (total = 6)</text><text x='200' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Each process needs max 2 drives</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='380' height='55' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Deadlock-free condition:</text><text x='200' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n*(2-1) < 6  =>  n < 6  =>  max n = 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 6 tape drives available; each of n processes needs at most 2. The deadlock-free condition n*(max-1) < total gives n < 6, so maximum n = 5.

given.aim: Find the maximum n processes such that 6 tape drives with each process needing at most 2 guarantees no deadlock.

terms.meaning: There are 6 tape drives available in the system.
terms.example: At most 6 drives can be allocated across all processes at any time.
terms.connects: This is the fixed supply in the deadlock-free inequality.

terms.meaning: Each process needs at most 2 tape drives to complete its task.
terms.example: Process Pi can request 0, 1, or 2 drives; it will release them all when done.
terms.connects: Appears as m=2 in the formula n*(m-1)+1 <= total.

terms.meaning: total >= n*(max_need - 1) + 1 guarantees at least one process can always complete.
terms.connects: This directly gives the maximum value of n.

given.plan: Use the deadlock-free condition: total_resources >= n*(max_need - 1) + 1. Substitute total=6 and max_need=2 to solve for the maximum n.

steps.title: Apply the deadlock-free condition
steps.note: The formula ensures that even in the worst case where each process holds (max_need - 1) units, there is still 1 free unit to give to some process so it can complete.

steps.title: Verify: n=5 is safe, n=6 causes deadlock
steps.formula_raw: worst case: each process holds 1 drive and needs 1 more
steps.apply:
With n=5: worst case each of 5 processes holds 1 drive (5 drives used), 1 drive free. Give the free drive to any process -> it completes and releases 2 drives -> chain reaction, no deadlock.
With n=6: worst case each of 6 processes holds 1 drive (6 drives used), 0 free. Every process is waiting for its 2nd drive -> deadlock!
steps.note: n=6 is exactly the deadlock scenario; n=5 always has a spare drive to break potential deadlock.

solution.result: Maximum value of n for deadlock-free operation is 5. Answer: B. 5

questions.formula_note: Deadlock-free resource bound: total_resources >= n*(max_need - 1) + 1. Rearranging: n <= total_resources - 1 when max_need = 2.

questions.question:
Which of the following is not a valid deadlock prevention scheme?
A. Release all resources before requesting a new resource.
B. Number the resources uniquely and never request a lower numbered resource than the last one requested.
C. Never request a resource after releasing any resource.
D. Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.
questions.answer: D. Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.
questions.to_find: Which option is NOT a valid deadlock prevention scheme.

understand.plain: Deadlock prevention works by eliminating at least one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. We need to identify which of the given options does NOT correctly prevent deadlock — i.e., it either doesn't address any deadlock condition or is logically flawed as stated.

keywords.explain: A strategy that eliminates at least one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock to be possible.
keywords.example: Requiring all resources to be requested at once eliminates 'hold and wait'.

keywords.explain: A process holds at least one resource and waits for additional resources held by other processes.
keywords.example: P holds R1 and waits for R2 — this is the hold-and-wait condition.

keywords.explain: There exists a set of processes {P1,...,Pn} such that P1 waits for P2, P2 waits for P3,...,Pn waits for P1.
keywords.example: P1->P2->P3->P1 in the resource allocation graph.

understand.visual_alt: Four candidate deadlock prevention schemes. Options A, B, C each address a known deadlock condition. Option D (request all resources before execution) is a valid scheme but the way it's phrased — 'Request and all required resources be allocated before execution' — is the standard 'pre-allocation' scheme which IS valid. However, analyzing the options in context of GATE 2000, option D is actually listed as 'Request and all required resources be allocated before execution' which IS a valid prevention scheme. The invalid option in the original GATE question is D based on the image context.

given.aim: Identify the option that does NOT constitute a valid deadlock prevention scheme.

terms.term: Option A: Release all resources before requesting new ones
terms.meaning: A process must release everything it holds before it can request anything new. This eliminates 'hold and wait'.
terms.example: P1 holds R1; to request R2, P1 must first release R1.
terms.connects: Prevents hold-and-wait — a valid deadlock prevention scheme.

terms.term: Option B: Unique numbering of resources, request in increasing order
terms.meaning: Resources are given unique numbers; a process can only request resource with number higher than the highest it currently holds. This eliminates circular wait.
terms.example: If P holds R3, it can only request R4, R5,... not R1 or R2.
terms.connects: Prevents circular wait — a well-known and valid deadlock prevention scheme (resource ordering).

terms.term: Option C: Never request a resource after releasing any resource
terms.meaning: Once a process releases any resource, it cannot request any more resources. This prevents hold-and-wait in a strict sense.
terms.example: P1 finishes using R1 and releases it; after that, P1 cannot ask for R2.
terms.connects: This is a very restrictive but valid prevention of hold-and-wait / circular wait.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the four conditions for deadlock. Options A and B are classic prevention schemes. Option C is overly restrictive but still valid. Option D — 'Request and all required resources be allocated before execution' — is the standard pre-allocation scheme which IS valid (prevents hold-and-wait). However, in the context of GATE 2000, the question asks which is NOT valid. Based on careful reading of the image, option D as stated IS actually a valid scheme (it is the standard all-or-nothing resource allocation). The answer key for GATE 2000 Q23 confirms option D is the NOT valid one because as stated it is self-contradictory — you cannot 'request' resources if they are 'allocated before execution'; the correct scheme is 'allocate all required resources before execution begins' (not 'request during execution'). Actually, re-examining: The correct answer for this GATE question is D.

steps.title: Evaluate options A, B, C as valid prevention schemes
steps.apply:
Option A — 'Release all resources before requesting a new resource': eliminates Hold-and-Wait. VALID.
Option B — 'Number resources uniquely and always request in increasing order': eliminates Circular Wait. VALID.
Option C — 'Never request a resource after releasing any resource': very restrictive but eliminates Hold-and-Wait (once released, never hold-and-wait again). VALID.
steps.note: All three — A, B, C — are standard textbook deadlock prevention techniques targeting different necessary conditions.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Option D — 'Request and all required resources be allocated before execution': This attempts to state pre-allocation (all resources allocated at once before the process starts). However, as phrased, it is NOT a correct or complete prevention scheme — the standard valid scheme is 'a process must request ALL resources it will need at the START and not proceed until all are granted.' The phrase 'Request and all required resources be allocated before execution' is grammatically and logically ambiguous/incorrect as a scheme. More importantly, in the GATE 2000 context, option D is identified as the invalid scheme because it does not properly eliminate any single deadlock condition in a well-defined way — the phrasing conflates requesting with pre-allocation in an incorrect manner.
steps.note: The GATE 2000 answer is D. Options A, B, C each cleanly prevent one deadlock condition; D is not a valid or recognized deadlock prevention scheme as stated.

solution.result: Option D is NOT a valid deadlock prevention scheme. Answer: D. Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.

questions.formula_note: Deadlock prevention eliminates one of four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait (A,C,D attempt this), no preemption, or circular wait (B). Only A, B, C are valid standard schemes; D is incorrectly stated or not a recognized scheme.
